summary: Jess Frazelle argues that achieving hard multi-tenancy in Kubernetes requires more than one layer of security, drawing parallels to how operating system kernels handle privilege separation. She proposes applying 'intra-kernel isolation' to Kubernetes, where each namespace gets its own nested Kubernetes system services (api-server, kube-proxy, etc.), so that exploiting one tenant's services doesn't compromise the entire cluster. The post outlines how combining namespace isolation with node-level resource control—or alternatively using VM-based container isolation—can provide defense in depth against both API vulnerabilities and container breakouts.
key_points: Current hard multi-tenancy proposals are insufficient because a single Kubernetes exploit leads to full cluster compromise
True sandboxes require multiple overlapping security layers—not just one boundary
Kubernetes namespaces should work like Solaris Zones or FreeBSD Jails, automatically providing full isolation
Each tenant namespace should get its own nested Kubernetes control plane components
Resource isolation should extend to node assignment, preventing tenants from sharing machines
VM-based containers (like katacontainers) can provide hardware-level isolation for better resource utilization
money_quotes: Kubernetes is the new kernel. We can refer to it as a 'cluster kernel' versus the typical operating system kernel.
All systems have weaknesses and nothing is perfect. With a system as complex and large as Kubernetes it is hard to trust the entire system to not be vulnerable.
One single exploit in Kubernetes leads to full supervisor privileges and then it's game over. This is not an acceptable way to secure a system and guarantee isolation between tenants.

These layers ensure that one vulnerability in the system does not take out the entire security of the system. We need this for hard multi-tenancy in Kubernetes as well. This is why all the existing proposals are insufficient.
The namespaces in Kubernetes should automatically set up a well isolated world, just like that of Zones or Jails without the user having to worry about if they configured it correctly.
Kubernetes by default shares everything and has many different, sometimes very broken, drivers and plugins just like that of an operating system kernel.
key_insight: Hard multi-tenancy in Kubernetes requires treating it like an operating system kernel and applying proven intra-kernel isolation techniques, giving each tenant their own nested control plane rather than relying on a single shared system that can be fully compromised by one exploit.
